annotations.label: Theoretical computer science

anchor.snippet: focuses on the abstract and mathematical foundations of computation

annotations.note: This is an informal description of an academic field, not a formalizable mathematical statement.

annotations.label: Gödel's incompleteness theorem

anchor.snippet: there are fundamental limitations on what statements could be proved or disproved

annotations.note: Mathlib only has Gödel's Beta Function Lemma (a stepping stone toward the First Incompleteness Theorem), not the incompleteness theorem itself.

annotations.label: Existence of NP-complete problems (Cook–Levin)

anchor.snippet: there exist practically relevant problems that are NP-complete

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of complexity classes P/NP or NP-completeness, so the Cook–Levin theorem is absent.

annotations.label: Algorithm

anchor.snippet: a step-by-step procedure for calculations

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes specific models of computation but no general informal notion of 'algorithm'.

annotations.label: Algorithm (effective method)

anchor.snippet: An algorithm is an effective method expressed as a finite list

annotations.note: The Church–Turing notion of effective method is captured by Mathlib's Turing machines and partial recursive functions, but no direct definition of 'algorithm' exists.

annotations.label: Randomized algorithm

anchor.snippet: some algorithms, known as randomized algorithms , incorporate random input

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of randomized algorithms.

annotations.label: Automata theory

anchor.snippet: the study of abstract machines and automata

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes abstract machines such as DFAs and NFAs, but not the field 'automata theory' as a defined object.

annotations.label: Coding theory

anchor.snippet: the study of the properties of codes and their fitness for a specific application

annotations.note: Mathlib has coding-theory infrastructure (Hamming distance/norm, Kraft–McMillan, uniquely decodable codes) but not a definition of the field itself.

annotations.label: Computational complexity theory

anchor.snippet: classifying computational problems according to their inherent difficulty

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of complexity classes or computational difficulty classification.

annotations.label: Computational geometry

anchor.snippet: the study of algorithms that can be stated in terms of geometry

annotations.note: No formalization of computational geometry algorithms exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Supervised learning

anchor.snippet: an algorithm is given samples that are labeled in some useful way

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of supervised learning or learning theory.

annotations.label: Mushroom classification

anchor.snippet: the samples might be descriptions of mushrooms, and the labels could be whether or not the mushrooms are edible

annotations.note: This is an informal illustrative example with no mathematical content to formalize.

annotations.label: Computational number theory

anchor.snippet: the study of algorithms for performing number theoretic computations

annotations.note: This is a field description rather than a formalizable statement, though number-theoretic objects are extensively formalized.

annotations.label: Integer factorization

anchor.snippet: The best known problem in the field is integer factorization

annotations.note: Mathlib defines prime factorization of naturals (`Nat.primeFactorsList`) but not the computational factoring problem or its hardness.

annotations.label: Cryptography

anchor.snippet: the practice and study of techniques for secure communication in the presence of third parties

annotations.note: Mathlib has no cryptography formalization.

annotations.label: Information-theoretic security (one-time pad)

anchor.snippet: information-theoretically secure schemes that provably cannot be broken even with unlimited computing power

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize cryptographic security notions such as the one-time pad.

annotations.label: Data structure

anchor.snippet: a particular way of organizing data in a computer so that it can be used

annotations.note: This is a computer-science concept with no formalized definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Distributed system

anchor.snippet: components located on networked computers communicate and coordinate their actions by passing messages

annotations.note: Mathlib has no model of distributed systems.

annotations.label: Information-based complexity

anchor.snippet: studies optimal algorithms and computational complexity for continuous problems

annotations.note: No formalization of information-based complexity exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Formal methods

anchor.snippet: the specification , development and verification of software and hardware systems

annotations.note: Mathlib is a mathematics library and does not formalize the field of formal methods itself.

annotations.label: Information theory

anchor.snippet: involving the quantification of information

annotations.note: Mathlib has an InformationTheory directory (Kullback–Leibler divergence, Hamming, binary entropy) but no defined notion of the field or a general Shannon entropy of a random variable.

annotations.label: Machine learning

anchor.snippet: the construction and study of algorithms that can learn from data

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of machine learning.

annotations.label: Natural computing

anchor.snippet: a terminology introduced to encompass three classes of methods

annotations.note: No formalization of natural computing exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Zuse-Fredkin thesis

anchor.snippet: the entire universe is a huge cellular automaton which continuously updates its rules

annotations.note: This philosophical thesis is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parallel computing

anchor.snippet: a form of computation in which many calculations are carried out simultaneously

annotations.note: Mathlib has no model of parallel computation.

annotations.label: Amdahl's law

anchor.snippet: The maximum possible speed-up of a single program as a result of parallelization is known as Amdahl's law

annotations.note: A grep for 'Amdahl' returned no results; this law is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Programming language theory

anchor.section: Programming language theory and program semantics
anchor.snippet: the design, implementation, analysis, characterization, and classification of programming languages

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize programming language theory.

annotations.label: Program semantics

anchor.section: Programming language theory and program semantics
anchor.snippet: semantics is the field concerned with the rigorous mathematical study of the meaning of programming languages

annotations.note: No formalization of program semantics exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Quantum computer

anchor.snippet: a computation system that makes direct use of quantum-mechanical phenomena

annotations.note: Mathlib has no model of quantum computation.

annotations.label: Symbolic computation

anchor.snippet: the study and development of algorithms and software for manipulating mathematical expressions and other mathematical objects

annotations.note: This describes a software/algorithmic field not formalized as a definition in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Very-large-scale integration

anchor.snippet: the process of creating an integrated circuit (IC) by combining thousands of transistors into a single chip

annotations.note: This is a hardware engineering concept with no presence in Mathlib.
